hub_subtitle: Business English para seguros, reuniões e entrevistas — e o desbloqueio da fala, do primeiro dia ao último

lesson.menu_title: What Time Is It? -- My Week, My Schedule
lesson.menu_desc: A aula em que ela deixa de responder "quando" e passa a NEGOCIAR quando. Seis palavras da agenda (schedule, morning, afternoon, appointment, deadline, busy) e as mesmas três palavrinhas da aula 5 -- at, on, in -- fazendo outro trabalho: agora dizem TEMPO. A escada: at nine (hora) → on Monday (dia) → in November (mês). E a frase que ela mais precisa e menos consegue dizer: "I'm busy on Monday. Are you free on Tuesday?" -- um não com uma porta aberta. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 6 -- What Time Is It?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Paula Morales | Lesson 6 -- What Time Is It?
lesson.grammar_point: prepositions of time (at / on / in) -- clock times, days of the week and months

listenings.text: Hi, Paula. David here. Your first week is in your schedule now, but let me tell you the important parts. On Monday you start at nine, and the welcome meeting is at nine thirty. Sofia is busy in the morning — she reads the reports — so she comes at two, in the afternoon. On Tuesday you visit a broker with her. You leave at eight, early. You come back in the afternoon, and I book us for four. And the deadline: your first report goes to Sofia on Friday, at five. Not on Monday. One more thing, and this is important: if your schedule is full, say it. "I'm busy" is a normal answer here. Nobody is angry. See you on Monday, at nine.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here, about your week and about mine. My schedule is open — look at it, and book me. If I am busy, book the next day; I am not going anywhere. In the morning I read reports, so book me in the afternoon — after two is better. Now, the big one: in November we travel to Miami. That is far, and it is not your problem this week. Your problem this week is Friday, at five: the deadline for your first report. Small things first. And Paula, one more thing. In twenty years, nobody made a good report at midnight. Send it on Friday at five, and go home. See you on Monday.

reading.rtitle: Your First Week — Commercial Team, São Paulo
paras: Welcome, Paula. This page is your first week, hour by hour. Read it on Sunday night, and come on Monday with your questions.

Monday. You start at nine. The welcome meeting is at nine thirty, in the meeting room on the second floor. Sofia is busy in the morning — she reads the reports — so she comes at two, in the afternoon. Tuesday. Your first broker visit, with Sofia. You leave the office at eight, in the morning, and you come back in the afternoon.

Wednesday is free — no appointments. Thursday you have a client call at eleven. And Friday is the day: your first report goes to Sofia on Friday, at five. Not on Saturday, not in the next week. On Friday, at five. In November we travel to Miami, but that is far. Friday is close.

This is Sofia, in her own words: "My schedule is public. Look at it, and book me — in the afternoon, after two. If I am busy, book the next day. And one more thing, Paula: 'I am busy' is a complete answer here. You don't have to say yes to every appointment. In twenty years, nobody made a good report at midnight."

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The page says Paula is late, and her first week starts on Friday.
The page is Paula's first week, hour by hour — when everything happens, and what is due on Friday at five.
The page explains that Sofia has no time for Paula this week.

items: The welcome meeting is at nine thirty, on Monday.
t
The text says it: "Monday. You start at nine. The welcome meeting is at nine thirty, in the meeting room on the second floor."
Sofia comes to the office in the morning on Monday.
f
No — she is busy in the morning, because she reads the reports. She comes at two, in the afternoon.
Paula's first broker visit is on Tuesday, at eight.
t
"Tuesday. Your first broker visit, with Sofia. You leave the office at eight, in the morning."
The deadline for the first report is on Saturday.
f
No. It is on Friday, at five. The text is clear: "Not on Saturday, not in the next week. On Friday, at five."
Sofia says Paula has to say yes to every appointment.
f
She says the opposite: "'I am busy' is a complete answer here. You don't have to say yes to every appointment."

bank.label: Asking / Perguntar
items: What time is the meeting?
Are you free on Tuesday?
What time works for you?
Can we meet in the afternoon?
Is the deadline on Friday?

bank.label: Answering / Responder
items: I have a call at ten, on Monday.
I'm busy on Monday. Are you free on Tuesday?
My appointment is in the afternoon.
The deadline is on Friday, at five.
That works for me.

items.situation: A colleague asks: "What time is the welcome meeting?" It is at nine thirty, on Monday. Answer with the full sentence.
tips: The welcome meeting is at nine thirty.
It's at nine thirty, on Monday.

items.situation: David wants thirty minutes on Monday. Your Monday is full. Say no — and offer another day in the same breath.
tips: I'm busy on Monday. Are you free on Tuesday?
Monday is full. Can we meet on Wednesday?

items.situation: Someone asks: "Is the deadline on Saturday?" It is on Friday, at five. Say no with the short answer first, then say when.
tips: The deadline is on Friday, at five.

items.situation: You want to know when the broker visit is. Ask the question — and ask for the time, not the day.
tips: What time is the broker visit?

items.situation: Sofia reads reports in the morning, so the morning is bad for her. Ask her for a time that works.
tips: Are you free in the afternoon?
Can we meet at three, on Wednesday?

items.situation: Tell a colleague your week in two sentences: one thing you have on Monday, and your deadline.
tips: I have a meeting at nine thirty, on Monday.
The deadline is on Friday, at five.
